title: Share of deaths from suicide

caption:
Share of deaths from suicide
Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.

Across seven countries between 1980 and 2023, the estimated share of deaths from suicide varies widely, with the vertical axis measuring the percentage of total deaths attributed to suicide from 0% to over 6%. The data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting. South Korea displays the most volatile trajectory, starting near 2.3% in 1980, rising steadily to a peak above 6% around 2010, and subsequently falling to approximately 4% by 2023. In contrast, the United States line shows a gradual upward trend, beginning near 1.5% and climbing to just under 2% by the end of the period. Japan maintains a relatively stable path, fluctuating between 2.5% and 3.5% for most of the timeline before dipping below 2% in recent years. Germany and the United Kingdom occupy the lower-middle range, with Germany hovering around 1.5% and the United Kingdom remaining near 1%. Peru and Greece represent the lowest values; Peru rises slightly from near 0% to roughly 0.7%, while Greece stays consistently flat near 0.4%. The lines show distinct national patterns, with South Korea reaching the highest visible values while Peru and Greece remain at the bottom of the scale throughout the observed timeframe.
